subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the correct statement for B2H6 from those given below.

(A) In B2H6, all B-H bonds are equivalent.

(B) In B2H6, there are four 3-centre- 2-electron bonds.

(C) B2H6 is a Lewis acid.

(D) B2H6 can be synthesized from both BF3 and NaBH4.

(E) B2H6 is a planar molecule.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Identify the correct statement for B2H6 from those given below.

(A) In B2H6, all B-H bonds are equivalent.

(B) In B2H6, there are four 3-centre- 2-electron bonds.

(C) B2H6 is a Lewis acid.

(D) B2H6 can be synthesized from both BF3 and NaBH4.

(E) B2H6 is a planar molecule.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
solution:
B2H6 have four 2c-2e bonds and two 3c-2e bonds. Bridging bonds have larger bond length than terminal bonds. Angle between terminal bonds is more than angle between bridging bonds if all 4 terminal bonds are in one plane then bridging bonds are in perpendicular plane.

3NaBH4+4BF3⟶ether450 K3NaBF4+2B2H6

Therefore we can say that only statement (C) and (D) are correct.

subject: Chemistry
q: The relative stability of +1 oxidation state of group 13 elements follows the order
question: The relative stability of +1 oxidation state of group 13 elements follows the order
solution:
Due to inert pair effect, the stability of +1 oxidation

state increases down the group. Thus, correct order of stability is $\mathrm{Al}  < \mathrm{Ga} < \mathrm{In} < \mathrm{Tl}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The incorrect statement about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> is :
question: The incorrect statement about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> is :
options: All <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> angles are of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo>°</mo></math>

The two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> bonds (bridged and terminal) are not of same length.

Terminal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> bonds have less <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></math> -character when compared to bridging bonds.
Its fragment, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> behaves as a Lewis acid.
solution:
θ2>θ1,    ∴B-H (terminal) having less p
character as compare to bridge bond.

Both B-H-B (bridge bond and terminal bond) do not have same bond length.

B-H-B bond angle is ≈90°

BH3 is electron deficient  species and therefore acts as Lewis acid

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The number of pairs among $\left[\mathrm{SiO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{SnO}, \mathrm{SnO}_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{PbO}, \mathrm{PbO}_{2}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{GeO}, \mathrm{GeO}_{2}\right]$, which contain oxides that are both amphoteric is 2.
Statement II: $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is an electron deficient molecule, can act as a Lewis acid, forms adduct with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and has a trigonal planar geometry.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The number of pairs among $\left[\mathrm{SiO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{SnO}, \mathrm{SnO}_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{PbO}, \mathrm{PbO}_{2}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{GeO}, \mathrm{GeO}_{2}\right]$, which contain oxides that are both amphoteric is 2.
Statement II: $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is an electron deficient molecule, can act as a Lewis acid, forms adduct with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and has a trigonal planar geometry.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I: Checking amphoteric nature of oxide pairs:
$[SiO_2, CO_2]$: Both acidic (non-metal oxides)
$[SnO, SnO_2]$: Both amphoteric
$[PbO, PbO_2]$: $PbO$ is amphoteric but $PbO_2$ is predominantly acidic/oxidizing
$[GeO, GeO_2]$: Both amphoteric (Ge is metalloid)
2 pairs have both oxides amphoteric. Statement I is true.
Statement II: $BF_3$ has B with 6 valence electrons (electron deficient), acts as Lewis acid, forms $BF_3 \cdot NH_3$ adduct, has $sp^2$ hybridization giving trigonal planar geometry. Statement II is true.

subject: Chemistry
q: Structures of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BeCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in solid state, vapour phase and at very high temperature respectively are:
question: Structures of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BeCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in solid state, vapour phase and at very high temperature respectively are:
solution:
BeCl2 is dimeric in vapour phase. The coordination number of beryllium is 3.

BeCl2 is monomeric at high temperature. The coordination number of beryllium is 2.

BeCl2 is polymeric in solid state. The coordination number of beryllium is 4.

subject: Chemistry
q: Borazine, also known as inorganic benzene, can be prepared by the reaction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-equivalents of "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>" with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math>-equivalents of "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>". "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>" and "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>", respectively are
question: Borazine, also known as inorganic benzene, can be prepared by the reaction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-equivalents of "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>" with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math>-equivalents of "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>". "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>" and "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>", respectively are
solution:
Reaction of ammonia with diborane gives initially B2H6.2NH3 which is formulated as BH2NH32+ BH4- ; further heating gives borazine, B3N3H6 known as ''inorganic benzene'' in view of its ring structure with alternate BH and NH groups.

3B2H6+6NH3→3BH2NH32+BH4-→Heat2B3N3H6+12H2

subject: Chemistry
q: Given below are the atomic numbers of some group 14 elements. The atomic number of the element with lowest melting point is :
question: Given below are the atomic numbers of some group 14 elements. The atomic number of the element with lowest melting point is :
solution:
Order of M.P. of group 14: $\mathrm{C}>\mathrm{Si}>\mathrm{Ge}>\mathrm{Pb}>\mathrm{Sn}$

$\begin{array}{ll}
\text { element } & \text { M.P. }\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right) \\
\mathrm{Z}=6=\mathrm{C} & 3730 \\
\mathrm{Z}=14=\mathrm{Si} & 1410 \\
\mathrm{Z}=32=\mathrm{Ge} & 937 \\
\mathrm{Z}=50=\mathrm{Sn} & 232 \\
\mathrm{Z}=82=\mathrm{Pb} & 327
\end{array}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: Group 13 trivalent halides get easily hydrolysed by water due to their covalent nature.

Statement II: AlCl3 upon hydrolysis in acidified aqueous solution forms octahedral AlH2O63+ ion.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: Group 13 trivalent halides get easily hydrolysed by water due to their covalent nature.

Statement II: AlCl3 upon hydrolysis in acidified aqueous solution forms octahedral AlH2O63+ ion.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is true but statement II is false
Statement I is false but statement II is true
Both statement I and statement II are false
Both statement I and statement II are true
solution:
In trivalent state most of the 13th group compounds being covalent are hydrolysed in water. Trichlorides of group 13 on hydrolysis in water form tetrahedral MOH4− species, the hybridisation state of element M is sp3.

In case of aluminium, acidified aqueous solution forms octahedral AlH2O63+ ion, which has an aluminium as a central metal atom and water molecules as ligands. The aluminium is in +3  oxidation state and forms 6 sp3d2 hybrid orbitals. 

Hence option D is the answer.

question:

Statement II : Silicon forms both electron rich and electron deficient hydrides.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both the statements I and II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is correct
Both the statements I and II are incorrect

solution:
Statement-I is correct.
Ni(Nickel) is used in Hydrogenation of unsaturated fat to make edible fats to convert unsaturated compounds to saturated compounds.

Statements-II is false as hydride of Silicon is electron precise & neither electron deficient nor electron rich.

q: On heating compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> gives a gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced></math> which is constituent of air. This gas when treated with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in the presence of a catalyst gives another gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mfenced></math> which is basic in nature. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> should not be :
question: On heating compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> gives a gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced></math> which is constituent of air. This gas when treated with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in the presence of a catalyst gives another gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mfenced></math> which is basic in nature. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> should not be :
solution:
PbNO32⟶ΔPbO+2NO2

N2 gas is constituent of air and it produces ammonia gas (basic nature) after reacting with H2 gas.

NH4NO2, (NH4)2Cr2O7 and NaN3 give nitrogen gas on heating but lead nitrate doesn't give nitrogen gas so it will be answer.

NH4NO2⟶ΔN2+2H2O

NH42Cr2O7⟶ΔN2+Cr2O3+H2O

NaN3⟶300°C3N2+2Na

subject: Chemistry
q: The Lewis acid character of boron tri halides follows the order:
question: The Lewis acid character of boron tri halides follows the order:
solution:
The strength of the acidic character of boron trihalides depends upon π-π back bonding. In boron trihalides, π-π -back bonding occurs due to empty orbital of boron and filled orbitals of halogens.

 

The π-π back bonding is shown maximum byBF3, as the size of B and F are small and comparatively same. Due to this effect tendency of accepting lone-pair of electrons of boron decreases as the size of halogen increases. The order of size of halogens are F < Cl < Br < I . Thus, acidic nature is in order BF3<BCl3<BBr3<Bl3.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The chlorides of Be and Al have Cl-bridged structure. Both are soluble in organic solvents and act as Lewis bases.

Statement II: Hydroxides of Be and Al dissolve in excess alkali to give beryllate and aluminate ions. In the light of the above statements. Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The chlorides of Be and Al have Cl-bridged structure. Both are soluble in organic solvents and act as Lewis bases.

Statement II: Hydroxides of Be and Al dissolve in excess alkali to give beryllate and aluminate ions. In the light of the above statements. Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both statement I and Statement II are true
Both statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
The chlorides of both beryllium and aluminium have Cl– bridged chloride structure in vapour phase. Both the chlorides are soluble in organic solvents and are strong Lewis acids. They are used as Friedel Craft catalysts.

Be2Cl4 is lewis acid and Al2Cl6 has complete octet.

Be and Al are amphoteric metals therefore dissolve in acid as well as alkaline solution and form beryllate and aluminate ions in excess alkali.

 Beryllium hydroxide dissolves in excess of alkali to give a beryllate ion,[Be(OH)4 ]2– just as aluminium hydroxide gives aluminate ion, [AlH2O2(OH)4]-

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following reactions
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}$
$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Y} \xrightarrow{\text { Non-Luminous flame }} \mathrm{Z}+\mathrm{SO}_{3}$
$2 \mathrm{Z}+2 \mathrm{X}+$ Carbon $\xrightarrow{\text { Luminous flame }} 2 \mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7}+\mathrm{CO}$
The oxidation states of Cu in Z and Q, respectively are :
question:
Consider the following reactions
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}$
$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Y} \xrightarrow{\text { Non-Luminous flame }} \mathrm{Z}+\mathrm{SO}_{3}$
$2 \mathrm{Z}+2 \mathrm{X}+$ Carbon $\xrightarrow{\text { Luminous flame }} 2 \mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7}+\mathrm{CO}$
The oxidation states of Cu in Z and Q, respectively are :
solution:
The given reactions describe the Borax Bead Test.
When borax ($Na_2B_4O_7 \cdot 10H_2O$) is heated, it first loses water and then melts to form a transparent glassy bead consisting of sodium metaborate ($NaBO_2$) and boric anhydride ($B_2O_3$).
$Na_2B_4O_7 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2NaBO_2 (X) + B_2O_3 (Y)$
In the non-luminous (oxidizing) flame, $B_2O_3$ reacts with $CuSO_4$ to form copper(II) metaborate ($Z$), which is blue in color.
$CuSO_4 + B_2O_3 \xrightarrow{Non-Luminous} Cu(BO_2)_2 (Z) + SO_3$
In $Cu(BO_2)_2$, the oxidation state of $Cu$ is $+2$.
In the luminous (reducing) flame, the copper(II) metaborate is reduced by carbon to copper(I) metaborate ($Q$), which is colorless/red.
$2Cu(BO_2)_2 (Z) + 2NaBO_2 (X) + C \xrightarrow{Luminous} 2CuBO_2 (Q) + Na_2B_4O_7 + CO$
In $CuBO_2$, the oxidation state of $Cu$ is $+1$.
Therefore, the oxidation states of $Cu$ in $Z$ and $Q$ are $+2$ and $+1$ respectively.

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct order of catenation is:
question: The correct order of catenation is:
solution:
Carbon atom have the tendency to link with one another through covalent bonds to form chains because of its tetra valency. This property is called catenation tendency. Carbon shows maximum catenation tendency among its group elements due to its high bond enthalpy. As we go down, the group size increases and electronegativity decreases therefore catenation tendency decreases.
 So,order of catenation tendency is 

C>Si>Ge≈Sn

question: The number of pentagons in <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>60</mn></mrow></msub></math> and trigons (triangles) in white phosphorus, are;
solution:
C60 is a molecule that consists of 60-C atoms, arranged as 12 pentagons and 20 hexagons.


It has 4 trigons.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: Elements ' $X$ ' and ' $Y$ ' are the most and least electronegative elements, respectively among $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{As}, \mathrm{Sb}$ and P. The nature of the oxides $\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is acidic and amphoteric, respectively.
Statement II : $\quad \mathrm{BCl}_{3}$ is covalent in nature and gets hydrolysed in water. It produces $\left[\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]^{-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ in aqueous medium.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: Elements ' $X$ ' and ' $Y$ ' are the most and least electronegative elements, respectively among $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{As}, \mathrm{Sb}$ and P. The nature of the oxides $\mathrm{X}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is acidic and amphoteric, respectively.
Statement II : $\quad \mathrm{BCl}_{3}$ is covalent in nature and gets hydrolysed in water. It produces $\left[\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]^{-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ in aqueous medium.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
For Statement I:
Among N, As, Sb, P, the electronegativity order is N > P > As > Sb. Thus X = N (most electronegative) and Y = Sb (least electronegative). $N_2O_3$ is acidic (forms HNO₂ with water) and $Sb_2O_3$ is amphoteric (reacts with both acids and bases).
Statement I is true.
For Statement II:
While $BCl_3$ is covalent and hydrolyzes in water to form $B(OH)_3$, the hydrolysis products are incorrect. In neutral aqueous medium, $B(OH)_3$ is formed. The tetrahedral $[B(OH)_4]^-$ forms only in basic conditions, and boron does not form $[B(H_2O)_6]^{3+}$ (boron is not a typical transition metal).
Statement II is false.

solution:

Caesium is used in devising photoelectric cells. Boron fibres are used in making bullet-proof vest. Silicones being surrounded by non-polar alkyl groups are water repelling in nature. Gallium is less toxic and has a very high boiling point, so it is used in high temperature thermometers.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement (I) : SiO2 and GeO2 are acidic while SnO and PbO are amphoteric in nature.
Statement (II) : Allotropic forms of carbon are due to property of catenation and pπ-dπ bond formation.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement (I) : SiO2 and GeO2 are acidic while SnO and PbO are amphoteric in nature.
Statement (II) : Allotropic forms of carbon are due to property of catenation and pπ-dπ bond formation.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is true but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I,

SnO, PbO, SnO2, PbO2 : Amphoteric CO2, SiO2, GeO2, GeO : Acidic CO : Neutral

According to Statement II,
Carbon does not have d-orbitals so can not form pπ-dπ Bond with itself. Due to properties of catenation and pπ-pπ bond formation, carbon is able to show allotropic forms. Allotropic forms of carbon can be crystalline and amorphous.
Diamond and graphite are two well-known crystalline forms of carbon. 

Hence option C is the answer.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Boron is extremely hard indicating its high lattice energy.

Statement II: Boron has highest melting and boiling point compared to its other group members.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Boron is extremely hard indicating its high lattice energy.

Statement II: Boron has highest melting and boiling point compared to its other group members.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Both statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
solution:
Boron is extremely hard as it exists as B12 icosahedral molecular units which accounts for its high lattice energy. So, statement-I is correct.

Due to small atomic size boron forms strong covalent bonds with the neighbouring atoms. Thus, boron atoms are closely packed in its solid, thus boron has high melting and boiling points. So, statement-II is also correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): In expensive scientific instruments, silica gel is kept in watch-glasses or in semipermeable membrane bags.

Reason (R): Silica gel adsorbs moisture from air via adsorption, thus protects the instrument from water corrosion (rusting) and / or prevents malfunctioning.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): In expensive scientific instruments, silica gel is kept in watch-glasses or in semipermeable membrane bags.

Reason (R): Silica gel adsorbs moisture from air via adsorption, thus protects the instrument from water corrosion (rusting) and / or prevents malfunctioning.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
The moist air becomes dry in presence of silica gel because the water molecules present in air get adsorbed on the surface of the gel. Silica gel adsorbs moisture from air via adsorption, thus protects the instrument from water corrosion (rusting) and/or prevents malfunctioning.

So, both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.

subject: Chemistry
q: The <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> bond length is maximum in:
question: The <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> bond length is maximum in:
solution:
In diamond, all carbons are sp3 hybridised and hence bond lengths are similar to carbon-carbon single bonds.
Graphite, C70 and C60 forms of carbon have delocalisation of electrons which leads to double bond characteristics. Hence, their bond lengths are in between carbon-carbon single and double bonds.
Hence, diamond with only single bond character will be have the longest bond.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct statements from the following are :
(A) The decreasing order of atomic radii of group 13 elements is \(\mathrm{Tl}>\) In \(>\mathrm{Ga}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{B}\).
(B) Down the group 13 electronegativity decreases from top to bottom.
(C) \(\mathrm{Al}\) dissolves in dil. \(\mathrm{HCl}\) and liberates \(\mathrm{H}_2\) but conc. \(\mathrm{HNO}_3\) renders \(\mathrm{Al}\) passive by forming a protective oxide layer on the surface.
(D) All elements of group 13 exhibits highly stable +1 oxidation state.
(E) Hybridisation of \(\mathrm{Al}\) in \(\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}\) ion is \(\mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2\).
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
The correct statements from the following are :
(A) The decreasing order of atomic radii of group 13 elements is \(\mathrm{Tl}>\) In \(>\mathrm{Ga}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{B}\).
(B) Down the group 13 electronegativity decreases from top to bottom.
(C) \(\mathrm{Al}\) dissolves in dil. \(\mathrm{HCl}\) and liberates \(\mathrm{H}_2\) but conc. \(\mathrm{HNO}_3\) renders \(\mathrm{Al}\) passive by forming a protective oxide layer on the surface.
(D) All elements of group 13 exhibits highly stable +1 oxidation state.
(E) Hybridisation of \(\mathrm{Al}\) in \(\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}\) ion is \(\mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2\).
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
A. size order $\mathrm{T} \ell>\mathrm{In}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Ga}>\mathrm{B}$ B. Electronegativity order $\mathrm{B}>\mathrm{Al} < \mathrm{Ga} < \mathrm{In} < \mathrm{T} \ell$ D. $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{Al}$ are more stable in +3 oxidation state
So, only C, E statements are correct.

subject: Chemistry
q: Identify the reaction which does not liberate hydrogen
question: Identify the reaction which does not liberate hydrogen
options: Reaction of zinc with aqueous alkali.
Allowing a solution of sodium in liquid ammonia to stand.
solution:
LiH react with B2H6 to produce boron lithium hydride.
The reaction proceeds in the medium of ester and complex form.

2LiH+B2H6→2Li[BH4]

So do not liberate H2 gas.

question: The number of <math><mn>2</mn></math>-centre-<math><mn>2</mn></math>-electron and <math><mn>3</mn></math>-centre-<math><mn>2</mn></math>-electron bonds in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math>, respectively, are:
